stdout:
OBSERVATIONS:
- Image 1, category 2: the raised texture forms an almost perfectly repeating lattice of round/hex bumps with constant spacing and amplitude — real rolled cathedral glass ripple is irregular in pitch and height; this looks like a tiled/procedural bump pattern.
- Image 1, category 3: the embossed bumps carry only a grayscale shading value (lighter on bump crests, darker in troughs) that sits uniformly on top of the purple→red gradient, rather than the gradient itself bending or intensifying with local thickness — the color layer and the relief layer look decoupled, as if a normal map were composited over a flat tint rather than both arising from one physical surface.
- Image 1, category 6: the entire frame is uniformly soft with no visible grain, no chromatic fringing at any bump edge, and no local sharp plane anywhere except the scribbled black mark — that mark has a hard, crisp edge while the glass right behind it is mushy, which is inconsistent with a single camera focus/DOF falloff (a real lens would keep marker-on-surface and near-surface texture in comparable focus).
- Image 2, category 1: each embedded air bubble carries its own small distinct specular highlight dot, consistent with many tiny curved real-glass/air interfaces each catching the light source independently — hard to fake procedurally at this density and consistency.
- Image 2, category 2/3: the orange chip in the center has an irregular, feathered translucent bleed trailing downward with no sharp boundary, and color saturation visibly deepens where chips overlap or thicken — both consistent with real fused-glass pigment migration under heat.
- Image 2, category 6: the white background shows faint fine-grained mottling (sensor/backlight-panel noise) and the black cames/wires have slightly uneven width and soft ink-bleed edges rather than vector-perfect lines.

VERDICT: Image 1 synthetic -- its texture is too spatially regular and its shading/color layers appear decoupled (bump relief independent of the color gradient), plus the uniform, grain-free blur with a mismatched crisp surface mark is inconsistent with real camera optics.
